content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 12 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 106v–106r | **Source Images:** DEU_012_page_001.jpg, DEU_012_page_002.jpg, DEU_012_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_012_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** These are the statutes and ordinances you must be careful to follow in the land the LORD, the God of your ancestors, has given you to possess — for as long as you live on earth.

**2** Completely demolish every place where the nations you are about to drive out worshipped their gods — on the high mountains, on the hills, and under every spreading tree.

**3** Tear down their altars, smash their sacred pillars, burn their Asherah poles in the fire, hack down the carved images of their gods, and obliterate their name from that place.

**4** You must not worship the LORD your God in the way they worshipped theirs.

**5** Instead, seek out the place the LORD your God will choose from among all your tribes to put his name there as his dwelling. Go there,

**6** and bring there your burnt offerings and your sacrifices, your tithes and your contributions, your vow offerings and your freewill offerings, and the firstborn of your herds and your flocks.

**7** There you will eat before the LORD your God, and you and your households will celebrate because of everything your hands have worked for — because the LORD your God has blessed you.

**8** You must not do as we do here today, with everyone doing whatever seems right in their own eyes —

**9** for you have not yet come to the resting place and the inheritance that the LORD your God is giving you.

**10** But when you cross the Jordan and settle in the land the LORD your God is giving you as your inheritance, and when he gives you rest from all your enemies around you so that you are living in safety —

**11** then bring to the place the LORD your God will choose as a dwelling for his name everything I am commanding you: your burnt offerings and your sacrifices, your tithes and your contributions, and all the choice vow offerings you make to the LORD.

**12** Rejoice before the LORD your God — you, your sons and daughters, your male and female servants, and the Levites from your towns, since they have no portion or inheritance of their own.

**13** Be careful that you do not offer your burnt offerings anywhere you please —

**14** only at the place the LORD chooses in one of your tribal territories. There you must offer your burnt offerings, and there you must carry out everything I am commanding you.

**15** Nevertheless, you may slaughter and eat meat wherever you wish within any of your towns — as many as the LORD your God blesses you — just as you would eat gazelle or deer. Both the ritually unclean and the clean may eat of it.

**16** Only this: you must not eat the blood. Pour it out on the ground like water.

**17** But you may not eat within your own towns the tithe of your grain, wine, or olive oil; or the firstborn of your herds or flocks; or any of your vow offerings that you have pledged; or your freewill offerings; or your contributions.

**18** These you must eat before the LORD your God in the place the LORD your God will choose — you, your son and daughter, your male and female servant, and the Levite who is within your towns. And you will rejoice before the LORD your God in everything your hands work toward.

**19** Take care not to neglect the Levite for as long as you live in your land.

**20** When the LORD your God enlarges your territory as he has promised you, and you crave meat and say, "I want to eat meat," you may eat as much meat as you wish.

**21** If the place the LORD your God chooses to put his name is too far away from you, you may slaughter from your herd or flock that the LORD has given you, as I have commanded you, and you may eat within your towns as much as you wish.

**22** Eat it just as you would eat gazelle or deer — both the unclean and the clean may eat it alike.

**23** But be very firm on this point: do not eat the blood — for the blood is the life, and you must not eat the life with the meat.

**24** Do not eat it. Pour it out on the ground like water.

**25** Do not eat it, so that things will go well for you and for your children after you, for you will be doing what is right in the eyes of the LORD.

**26** But the sacred things you have and the things you have vowed to give — take them and go to the place the LORD will choose.

**27** Present your burnt offerings — the meat and the blood — on the altar of the LORD your God. The blood of your sacrifices must be poured out beside the altar of the LORD your God, but the meat you may eat.

**28** Be careful to obey all these words I am commanding you, so that things will always go well for you and for your children after you, for you will be doing what is good and right in the eyes of the LORD your God.

**29** When the LORD your God has cut off before you the nations that you are going in to drive out, and when you have driven them out and settled in their land,

**30** take care that you are not ensnared into imitating them after they have been destroyed before you. Do not inquire about their gods, saying, "How did these nations serve their gods? I will do the same."

**31** You must not do that to the LORD your God, because everything they do for their gods is detestable to the LORD — things he hates. They even burn their sons and daughters in the fire as offerings to their gods!

**32** Everything I am commanding you, be careful to do it. Do not add to it or subtract from it.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 2–3 — The total dismantling of Canaanite cultic sites:** The Hebrew verbs here cascade with destructive force: tear down (nattots), smash (shibar), burn (saraph), hew down (gada'), obliterate (ibedu). PNEUMA reproduces this cascade — "demolish," "tear down," "smash," "burn," "hack down," "obliterate" — to convey the absolute thoroughness of the command. The LXX uses similarly forceful Greek verbs. The Asherah poles were wooden cult symbols associated with the goddess Asherah; the sacred pillars (matsevot) were standing stones.

**V. 5 — "The place the LORD your God will choose":** Ha-maqom asher yivchar YHWH — this phrase recurs like a drumbeat throughout Deuteronomy 12–26. It is the theological center of the entire law code: centralized, chosen-place worship. The location is deliberately not named in Moses' address (Jerusalem is not yet conquered), signaling future fulfillment. PNEUMA renders "will choose" rather than "has chosen" to preserve this open-ended, future-oriented character. The LXX reads hon an eklextai kyrios ho theos hymon — "which the LORD your God might choose."

**V. 5 — "to put his name there as his dwelling":** The theology of the divine name (shem) residing at the sanctuary is characteristically Deuteronomic — sometimes called "Name Theology." Rather than God bodily present in the temple, the divine name is enthroned there. PNEUMA renders "dwelling" for shikno (his dwelling-place) to capture the residential permanence.

**V. 7–8 — "Everyone doing whatever seems right in their own eyes":** Kol ha-yashar be-einav — this is Israel's current wilderness condition, not necessarily a criticism of moral chaos (in context), but a description of the decentralized worship that must give way to unified national practice. PNEUMA renders it somewhat wryly: "everyone doing whatever seems right in their own eyes" — letting the reader feel the problem without over-editorializing.

**V. 12 — Rejoicing and inclusion of the Levite:** Three times in chapter 12 the Levite is explicitly included in covenant celebration (vv. 12, 18, 19). The Levite's landlessness (they inherited only the LORD) makes them dependent on the community's generosity. PNEUMA's rendering "Levites from your towns, since they have no portion or inheritance of their own" makes the logic of their inclusion explicit.

**V. 15–16 — The profane slaughter permission:** This is a significant legal development. Outside of sacrifice, any Israelite may slaughter for food anywhere — "secular" meat-eating (as opposed to sacrificial meals). The only restriction is blood. PNEUMA uses "ritually unclean and the clean" to indicate that the usual purity distinctions do not apply to this non-sacrificial meat. This represents a pastoral concession: not every meal must be a sacrifice.

**V. 23 — "The blood is the life":** Ki ha-dam hu ha-nefesh — nefesh is normally translated "soul" or "life." PNEUMA renders: "the blood is the life" — keeping nefesh as "life" to match its biological-vitalist meaning here. Blood prohibition is grounded not in ritual squeamishness but in the conviction that life belongs to God. To eat blood would be to consume life itself — which only God can own and give. The LXX reads hoti to haima auto psyche — "the blood itself is the soul/life."

**V. 28 — "What is good and right in the eyes of the LORD your God":** The pairing of tov (good) and yashar (right/straight) appears here as ethical summary. Moses tells Israel that covenant obedience is not arbitrary — it is the path of genuine human flourishing. PNEUMA renders "good and right" without conflating them.

**V. 30–31 — The danger of religious curiosity about pagan practice:** Moses warns against ethnographic curiosity becoming imitation: "How did these nations serve their gods?" The answer is given bluntly — they burn their children. Child sacrifice (the Molech cult) is named as the definitive marker of what the LORD detests. PNEUMA renders this with full force: "they even burn their sons and daughters in the fire as offerings to their gods!" — the exclamation mark reflecting the rhetorical horror of the Hebrew.

**V. 32 — "Do not add to it or subtract from it":** Lo tosef alav velo tigra mimenu — this closing formula (cf. also Deuteronomy 4:2) establishes the Torah as a complete, bounded body of instruction. PNEUMA renders straightforwardly. The LXX reads ou prostheseis ep' auto kai ouk aphairethesete ap' autou — you shall not add and you shall not take away.

— Signed, PNEUMA
